symbols: ornate golden halo — sanctity and divine favor upon the holy healer
red cap and hood — the physician's attire, identifying Cosmas as one of the healing saints
gold background — the light of eternity, the heavenly realm where the saints dwell
description: A profile portrait of a young clean-shaven man facing right, shown from the shoulders up. He wears a distinctive red hooded cap or coif over a blue-and-white patterned head covering. Behind his head is an elaborate golden halo with decorative punched patterns radiating outward. The background is a warm gold. The rendering is delicate and precise, with soft modeling of the face.
theological_description: Saint Cosmas, together with his twin brother Damian, belongs to the group known as the Anargyroi — 'the unmercenary ones' — physicians who healed without accepting payment, following Christ's command: 'Freely you have received; freely give' (Matthew 10:8). Their lives of gratuitous healing enacted the ministry of Jesus himself, who 'went about doing good and healing all who were oppressed by the devil' (Acts 10:38). Martyred during the Diocletian persecution (c. 303 AD), they became patron saints of physicians and surgeons. Their witness embodies James's teaching that 'faith without works is dead' (James 2:26) — their medical skill was exercised as an expression of Christian love, making the healing ministry of Christ tangible in the world.

scripture_refs.reason: Freely you have received, freely give — the basis for Cosmas and Damian's unmercenary healing
Jesus went about doing good and healing all — the model for the physician saints
Faith without works is dead — Cosmas embodied faith through healing action
